provisions.description: Establishes eligibility for Ukrainian parolees (citizens/nationals of Ukraine or persons who last habitually resided in Ukraine, paroled into the U.S. between 2/24/2022 and 9/30/2023, or later-paroled spouses, children, and parents/guardians of unaccompanied children) for resettlement assistance, entitlement programs, and other benefits available to refugees admitted under INA section 207, except initial resettlement under INA section 412(b)(1). Also eligible for unaccompanied alien child services. Excludes eligibility from Paperwork Reduction Act for translations. Section 421(a) of PRWORA does not apply. Does not confer lawful permanent resident status.

provisions.description: Subsection (a) amends section 2606(a) of the Ukraine Supplemental Appropriations Act, 2022 (division N of P.L. 117-103) to extend loan authority from fiscal year 2022 to fiscal years 2022 through 2024; also provides that FMF funds in this title shall be available for such loans. Subsection (b) authorizes Ukraine to use FMF in this Act and prior Acts for procurement of defense articles, services, or design/construction services not sold by USG under the Arms Export Control Act, during FY2022-2023, subject to section 38 notification requirements.

notes: Extends FMF loan authority and enables Ukraine to procure non-USG defense articles with FMF funds; significant policy expansion

provisions.description: Requires the Secretary of State and USAID Administrator to jointly report within 30 days on proposed uses of Title V funds (excluding Multilateral Assistance), with updates every 60 days until September 30, 2024 and every 120 days thereafter until all funds expended. The Secretary of the Treasury shall submit a separate report within 30 days for Multilateral Assistance funds on the same schedule.
deadlines: every 120 days thereafter until all funds expended

notes: Two parallel reporting requirements: State/USAID for most Title V funds and Treasury for Multilateral Assistance

provisions.description: Establishes conditions for direct financial support to the Government of Ukraine from ESF funds: (a) funds may be used for direct financial support, matched to the maximum extent practicable by non-USG sources; (b) subject to a memorandum of understanding with transparency and accountability safeguards, maintained in a separate auditable account; (c) requires reporting 15 days prior to initial obligation and every six months thereafter; (d) requires reporting on uses and results every 90 days through September 30, 2025; (e) subject to regular notification procedures of Appropriations Committees.
provisions.title: V

notes: Critical oversight provision governing direct cash transfers to the Government of Ukraine; includes matching requirement, MOU requirement, separate auditable account, and multiple reporting requirements

provisions.description: No part of any appropriation contained in this Act shall remain available for obligation beyond the current fiscal year unless expressly so provided.

flagged_issues: SEC. 203 increases DoD humanitarian assistance cap from $450M to $950M for FY2022
SEC. 501 increases Presidential drawdown authority from $100M to $11B for FY2022—a 110x increase
SEC. 502 increases multiple Presidential waiver authority caps under FAA section 614
SEC. 503 increases peacekeeping emergency fund from $25M to $100M for FY2022
SEC. 504 extends FMF loan authority through FY2024 and allows Ukraine non-AECA procurements
SEC. 603 is a death gratuity for Rep. Don Young, unrelated to Ukraine
O&M Defense-Wide ($15.256B) contains $6B USAI sub-allocation and $9.05B transfer authority ceiling, both with extended availability through FY2023
NRC Salaries and Expenses account name collides with DOJ Salaries and Expenses—different agencies in Titles I and III
Two separate Office of Inspector General accounts in Title V—one for State ($4M) and one for USAID ($1M)
